passages.explanation: Jesus goes to Jerusalem for a Jewish festival and visits the pool of Bethesda, where crowds of sick and disabled people waited for the water to be stirred — believed to be caused by an angel, with the first person in being healed. Jesus singles out one man who has been ill for 38 years. His question — 'Do you want to be made well?' — sounds obvious, but it forces the man to express what he actually wants rather than just passively waiting.

The man doesn't directly answer yes. Instead he explains his situation: he has no one to help him into the pool in time, and someone always gets there first. He's describing helplessness and isolation. Jesus bypasses the pool entirely — he doesn't tell the man to get into the water. He simply commands him to get up, pick up his mat, and walk. The man is healed instantly.

The healing happened on a Sabbath, and Jewish authorities immediately confront the man — not about the miracle, but about carrying his mat, which they consider work and therefore a Sabbath violation. The man points to Jesus as the one who told him to do it, but he doesn't actually know who Jesus is. Jesus had slipped away into the crowd. The authorities' concern is with the rule-breaking, not the fact that a man sick for 38 years is suddenly walking.

Jesus finds the man later in the temple and warns him to stop sinning or something worse may happen. The man then reports to the Jewish authorities that it was Jesus who healed him. This triggers persecution — they want to kill Jesus for two reasons: he broke the Sabbath, and when confronted about it, he called God 'my Father' and said he was doing the same work God does. They understood exactly what he was claiming: equality with God.

Jesus responds to the accusation with a long speech. He says the Son does nothing independently — he only does what he sees the Father doing. The Father shows the Son everything and will show him even greater things. Just as the Father raises the dead and gives life, the Son does the same. The Father has handed all judgment to the Son so that people will honour the Son the same way they honour the Father. Refusing to honour the Son means refusing to honour God.

Jesus says that anyone who hears his message and believes has already passed from death into life and will not face judgment. A time is coming when the dead — both spiritually and physically — will hear the Son's voice. Those who hear it will live. He has been given authority to judge because he is the Son of Man. Everyone in the grave will eventually hear his voice and be raised — some to life, some to judgment. Jesus insists his judgment is fair because he doesn't act on his own will but on the Father's.

Jesus acknowledges that self-testimony isn't convincing on its own, so he points to external witnesses. John the Baptist testified about him — and the authorities were willing to listen to John for a while. But Jesus says he has something greater than John's testimony: the works the Father gave him to do. These miracles themselves are evidence that the Father sent him. He then confronts them directly: they have never heard God's voice or seen his form, and God's word doesn't live in them — because they refuse to believe the one God sent.

Jesus tells them they search the Scriptures thinking that the texts themselves give eternal life — but those very Scriptures point to him, and they still refuse to come to him. He says he's not looking for human approval, but he knows them: they don't have God's love in them. They reject someone who comes in God's name but would accept someone who comes in their own name. Their problem is that they seek approval from each other rather than from God, and that makes genuine belief impossible.

Jesus closes by turning their own authority against them. They won't be accused by Jesus — they'll be accused by Moses, the very figure they claim to follow. If they actually believed what Moses wrote, they would believe Jesus, because Moses wrote about him. If they can't believe Moses' writings, they won't believe Jesus' words either. The argument puts them in a trap: rejecting Jesus means they were never truly following Moses in the first place.
